FILE: syncProductMaster_steps.txt

DATE: 30 Oct 2025

AUTHOR: Srijita

PURPOSE: Step-by-step documentation of the “syncProductMaster” API function

FUNCTION NAME:

syncProductMaster

DESCRIPTION:

This API synchronizes product master data from the SAP product master service to the local database.

It fetches the latest product details from the SAP system, transforms the data into the internal schema, validates reference data (categories, brands, suppliers, collections, sub-brands), and inserts or updates product records efficiently using batched operations.

The synchronization ensures consistency between the SAP product catalog and the local product master system, maintaining up-to-date product information for pricing, SKU management, and inventory operations.

The functionality includes:


- Date-based fetching of product records from SAP

- Duplicate filtering by product variant

- Data transformation and GST/UOM/points standardization

- Validation of mapping references (Category, Brand, Supplier, Collection, etc.)

- Bulk insertion and update in local MongoDB for efficient processing

- Controlled concurrency using lock management

STEP 1: Acquire Lock to Prevent Concurrent Processing


Lock Mechanism:

if (!(await acquireLock("syncProductMaster"))) {
  res.status(400);
  throw new Error("Another sync is in progress. Please try again later.");
}

Purpose:


- Prevents multiple simultaneous sync jobs that could lead to inconsistent or duplicated products.

- Ensures only one instance of the synchronization runs at a time.

Logging:

console.log("syncProductMaster: Started");

The lock is later released in the finally block to guarantee cleanup regardless of success or failure.

STEP 2: Handle Date Range and Validate Input


Default Date Configuration:

let currentDate = new Date();
currentDate = `${dd}.${mm}.${yyyy}`;
let previousDate = new Date();
previousDate = `${dd}.${mm}.${yyyy}`;

Both dates default to the current date (same day sync), formatted as DD.MM.YYYY.

Custom Range Allowed via Query Parameters:
if (req.query.previousDate && req.query.currentDate) {
  if (new Date(req.query.previousDate) > new Date(req.query.currentDate)) {
    throw new Error("Previous date cannot be greater than current date");
  } else {
    currentDate = req.query.currentDate;
    previousDate = req.query.previousDate;
  }
}

Purpose:


- Enables fetching data incrementally between defined SAP date ranges.

- Enforces valid date intervals avoiding reversed ranges.

Example:

?previousDate=28.07.2025&currentDate=30.07.2025

STEP 3: Fetch Product Data from SAP API


SAP API Endpoint:

const url =
  "http://182.75.250.216:8000/sap/opu/odata/sap/ZRUPA_DMS_PRODUCT_MASTER_SRV/headerSet";

  GET Request Example:

  const response = await axios.get(
  `${url}?sap-client=100&$filter=fromDate eq '${previousDate}' and toDate eq '${currentDate}' and variant eq ''&$format=json`,
  {
    headers: { Cookie: "sap-usercontext=sap-client=100" },
  }
);

Expected Data Example:

{
  "d": {
    "results": [
      {
        "variant": "RUPA001BOX",
        "description": "Classic Vest",
        "manufacturer": "RUPA",
        "size": "L",
        "color": "White",
        "cgst": "2.5",
        "sgst": "2.5",
        "igst": "",
        "points": "10"
      }
    ]
  }
}

Error Handling:


- If no data found, response is 404 with message "No data found".

STEP 4: Filter Unique Product Variants


Since duplicate variants may exist, the API filters to keep only the latest record per variant:

const uniqueVariants = new Set();
data = data.filter((item) => {
  const variant = item.variant || "";
  if (uniqueVariants.has(variant)) return false;
  uniqueVariants.add(variant);
  return true;
});

Purpose:

Ensures duplicate entries from SAP do not lead to redundant product insertions.

STEP 5: Define Helper Functions for Data Transformation


These internal methods normalize and standardize key attributes from raw SAP data.

Unit of Measure (UOM):

const getUOM = (item) => {
  const su = item.sales_unit?.trim();
  if (su === "BOX") return "box";
  if (su === "DZ") return "dz";
  if (["1PA", "2PA", "3PA", "PAA", "PAK"].includes(su)) return su;
  return "pcs";
};

Pieces in Box:
const getPcsInBox = (item) => {
  const pcs = Number(item.sales_conv?.replace(/[^0-9.]/g, "")) || 1;
  return pcs > 0 ? pcs.toString() : "1";
};

Base Points:
const getBasePoints = (item) => {
  const points = parseFloat(item.points?.replace(/[^0-9.]/g, "")) || 0;
  return points.toString();
};

Tax handling (GST):
const getGst = (item) => {
  let cgst = Number(item.cgst) || 0;
  let sgst = Number(item.sgst) || 0;
  let igst = Number(item.igst) || 0;

  if (igst && (!cgst || !sgst)) {
    cgst = sgst = igst / 2;
  } else if (!igst && (cgst || sgst)) {
    igst = (cgst || sgst) * 2;
  }

  return { cgst: `${cgst}`, sgst: `${sgst}`, igst: `${igst}` };
};

Supplier Code:
const getSupplierCode = (item) => {
  if (item.manufacturer?.trim()) return item.manufacturer.trim();
  if (item.plant?.trim()) return "C" + item.plant.trim();
  return "";
};

STEP 6: Transform SAP Data into Application Schema


Each record transformed into a normalized, full-fledged object aligning with the internal data model.

const transformedData = data.map((item) => ({
  "Product Code": item.variant || "",
  "Product Name": item.description || "",
  "SKU Group Code": item.base || "",
  "SKU Group Name": item.base_desc || "",
  "Product Size": item.size || "",
  "Product Color": item.color || "",
  "Supplier Code": getSupplierCode(item),
  CGST: getGst(item).cgst,
  SGST: getGst(item).sgst,
  IGST: getGst(item).igst,
  "Unit of Measure": getUOM(item),
  "Pieces in a box": getPcsInBox(item),
  "Base Points": getBasePoints(item),
}));

Purpose:

Converts all SAP product data into a clean, unified structure that matches the local product schema.

STEP 7: Extract Unique Master Codes


Build Unique Reference Sets:

const uniqueCodes = {
  categories: new Set(),
  collections: new Set(),
  brands: new Set(),
  subBrands: new Set(),
  products: new Set(),
};

Extract From Data:
transformedData.forEach((row) => {
  if (row["Category Code"]?.trim())
    uniqueCodes.categories.add(row["Category Code"].trim());
  // above repeated for all entities
});

Purpose:

Avoids repetitive DB calls by fetching relevant entities and suppliers only once per unique reference.

STEP 8: Fetch Existing Collections and Products

Query DB for References:

const [
  categories, collections, brands, subBrands, suppliers, existingProducts,
] = await Promise.all([
  Category.find({ code: { $in: Array.from(uniqueCodes.categories) } }),
  Collection.find({ code: { $in: Array.from(uniqueCodes.collections) } }),
  Brand.find({ code: { $in: Array.from(uniqueCodes.brands) } }),
  SubBrand.find({ code: { $in: Array.from(uniqueCodes.subBrands) } }),
  Supplier.find({ supplierCode: { $in: Array.from(uniqueSupplierCodes) } }),
  Product.find({ product_code: { $in: Array.from(uniqueCodes.products) } })
]);

Purpose:

- Preloads existing documents for relational validation.

- Prevents failed inserts due to missing FK references.

STEP 9: Batch Process and Validate Records

Process Data in Groups:
const BATCH_SIZE = 5000;
for (let i = 0; i < transformedData.length; i += BATCH_SIZE) {
  const batch = transformedData.slice(i, i + BATCH_SIZE);
  // Process insertions, updates, and skips
}

Validation Before Insert/Update:

For each product:


- Required fields: Product Code, Name, SKU Group, Category, Brand, Supplier, Collection

- References: Each must exist in DB (categoryMap, supplierMap, etc.)

- If missing → added to skipped list with descriptive “reason”.

STEP 10: Bulk Insert New Products


New (non-existing) product insert:
const result = await Product.insertMany(validProducts, { ordered: false });
totalInserted += result.length;

Error Handling:


- If duplicate key or insert failure occurs → log record with “Database insertion failed” reason.

STEP 11: Bulk Update Existing Products


Batch Update Logic:

Use optimized MongoDB bulkWrites:
const result = await Product.bulkWrite(
  updateBatch.map(({ filter, update }) => ({
    updateOne: { filter, update, upsert: false },
  })),
  { ordered: false }
);
totalUpdated += result.modifiedCount;

Purpose:

Batch updates ensure schema consistency without creating duplicates.

STEP 12: Track Batch Statistics and Skipped Rows

After processing each batch:
totalProcessed += batch.length;
totalSkipped += batchSkipped.length;
skippedRows.push(...batchSkipped);

console.log(`Batch summary: processed=${batch.length}, inserted=${validProducts.length}, updated=${productsToUpdate.length}, skipped=${batchSkipped.length}`);

STEP 13: Send Final Summary Response

Response Format:
return res.status(200).json({
  success: true,
  message: "Product master data synced successfully",
  data: {
    totalProcessed,
    totalInserted,
    totalUpdated,
    totalSkipped,
    skippedRows,
  },
});


Example Response:
{
  "success": true,
  "message": "Product master data synced successfully",
  "data": {
    "totalProcessed": 12000,
    "totalInserted": 4800,
    "totalUpdated": 7000,
    "totalSkipped": 200,
    "skippedRows": []
  }
}

STEP 14: Error Handling and Lock Release


Global error management with data integrity ensured:
catch (error) {
  console.log("syncProductMaster: Caught error", error);
  res.status(400);
  throw error;
} finally {
  await releaseLock("syncProductMaster");
  console.log("syncProductMaster: Lock released, finished");
}

FINAL OUTCOME


When executed successfully:


1. Synchronizes product master data from SAP system to local DB.

2. Ensures comprehensive transformation and validation for each record.

3. Performs high-speed batch insertion and updates.

4. Logs complete summary including processed, inserted, updated, and skipped counts.

5. Maintains SAP-ERP parity with local system with zero duplication and minimal downtime.

SUMMARY OF LOGICAL FLOW

1. Acquire synchronization lock.

2. Determine date range for data fetch.

3. Fetch product data from SAP OData API.

4. Filter duplicate variants.

5. Transform data fields and normalize structure.

6. Extract unique master codes for mapping.

7. Preload DB references (categories, brands, suppliers, collections, etc.).

8. Validate and group records in batches.

9. Insert new products and update existing ones in sub-batches of 1000 records.

10. Aggregate statistics across all batches.

11. Return summary JSON response with detailed counts.

12. Release lock regardless of success or failure.


IMPORTANT BUSINESS RULES

1. Single-job concurrency control: Only one product sync can be active at a time (syncProductMaster lock).

2. Date filtering: Data pulled only for defined or default date range (DD.MM.YYYY).

3. Unique variant enforcement: Duplicate product variants ignored.

4. Reference validation required: Category, brand, supplier, and collection must exist.

5. Tolerant inserts: Partial insert/update batches allowed (ordered: false).

6. High scalability: Process handled in large 5000-record batches subdivided into 1000 DB writes.

7. Comprehensive error tracking: Each skipped record logged with descriptive “reason.”

8. Performance-oriented mapping: Uses hash maps for constant-time relation lookups.

9. Consistent tax and UOM normalization across variants.

10. Guaranteed cleanup: Lock always released, preventing deadlocks.


---
AUTHOR: Srijita
DATE: 30 Oct 2025
